data.title: Invesco Core Plus Bond Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Potential to lock in yields

Bond yields, though down from their highs, are still at attractive levels compared to the last 10 years, in our view. Investors can lock in these yields for years to come by investing in high grade bonds before interest rates come down further.

2 Picking your spots

We believe numerous important factors enhance the attractiveness of investment grade credit. We observe that yield spreads between Treasuries and non-Treasury sectors have been tight, which makes our credit underwriting and security selection even more important.

3 Beating the crowd

Investors have amassed large amounts in cash and money markets (Source: St. Louis Fed. Data as of 12/31/25). As US Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cutting resumes and interest rates fall, cash will likely move to risk assets, pushing bond prices up and yields down. Timing a move is hard, but for now, investors are in our view being paid to wait in fixed income.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Market sentiment during the quarter appeared mixed. The US unemployment rate rose and consumer spending growth appears to have slowed, both likely affected by the longest government shutdown in US history. Still, overall economic growth was buoyed by investment in artificial intelligence ('AI') and related sectors. Corporate fundamentals remained firm following a strong earnings season and elevated capital market activity. However, a divergence of economic confidence between CEOs and consumers raised concerns about an economic divide between higher and lower income earners. AI “bubble” fears appeared to further dampen sentiment.
  • A weakening labor market led the Fed to resume rate cuts, reducing the federal funds rate twice, for a target range of 3.50-3.75%, the lowest since 2022. Markets expect two more rate cuts in 2026 as the Fed has expressed a desire to get interest rates close to a neutral stance. Credit spreads – the yield difference between Treasuries and US investment grade bonds – widened due to moderate volatility early in the quarter but have remained historically tight.
  • Our bond outlook is constructive. Market-implied recession probabilities in our view remain well below the expectations of surveyed economists, and interest rate stability, if it materializes, would likely further support demand. Our view is that growth will accelerate in 2026 but might face a headwind of elevated inflation early in the year.

Portfolio positioning

We maintain a positive outlook for investment grade credit and maintain the fund's overweight in the sector. Yield spreads between Treasuries and investment grade bonds have remained tight compared to past levels, but elevated yields appear to have continued to attract buyers. Based on the segment's health, we believe investment grade corporates remain attractive on a risk-adjusted basis. In our view, corporate fundamentals remain firm, the yield backdrop appears attractive and lower average dollar prices for bonds across the index present discounted buying opportunities, potentially enhancing downside protection for bondholders.

As the Fed's rate cutting has resumed, our perspective is that, in the current environment, the bond market benefits from rate cuts regardless of what drives them. If the Fed cuts because inflation has sustainably cooled, yield spreads between Treasuries and US investment grade bonds could remain tight or even compress further. Conversely, if cuts come in response to weaker growth, investment grade credit should still hold up – high-quality bonds generally become more valuable and income remains durable. Therefore, we have a constructive view of bonds in the intermediate part of the yield curve, which captures income and price appreciation potential while reducing excess duration risk.

Within the investment grade corporate segment, we maintain an overweight in financials, particularly in the banking sector. While valuations in the sector have narrowed toward long-term median levels, we believe the strength of corporate fundamentals still supports a positive outlook.

During the quarter, we decreased the fund's overweights in the high-yield corporate and emerging market debt segments, which are not in the benchmark, in order to reduce credit risk and in anticipation of a potentially robust new issuance calendar. Yields and income are in our view attractive; however, we believe diligence is critical given potential for heightened volatility. Additionally, we maintain an underweight in Agency mortgages because valuations have become less attractive to us.

We kept the fund's duration fairly neutral compared to the benchmark, lengthening it due to potential slowing of US growth and as a hedge to credit risk.

data.title: GE Vernova: Repowering America
data.description:
GE Vernova (GEV) is positioned to accelerate growth in the coming years in Power and Electrification as market demand drives substantial growth in new, margin-accretive orders. In addition to expanding production capacity, GE Vernova should see strong tailwinds for profitability over the coming years as the firm expands its serviceable power and electrical equipment footprint, driving recurring revenue through long-term service agreements. With new gas turbine capacity expected to come online in eq3’26 and the addition of Prolec GE to Electrification, I believe GE Vernova is well positioned to realize durable growth over the coming years; I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for GEV shares with a price target of $1,169/share at 59.15x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

GE Vernova reported an upbeat close to FY25 with a 50% sequential increase in quarterly orders, ending q4’25 with $22.26b in new orders for a total book-to-bill ratio of 2.03x; GE Vernova closed FY25 with $150b in backlog. Growth in orders was underpinned by strength in the Electrification and Gas markets, with Electrification printing a book-to-bill ratio of 2.51x. The Wind segment even realized revitalization in the quarter with a book-to-bill ratio of 1.33x, up from a declining order book since q3’24.

GE Vernova Operational Update

Power Segment

Orders in the power segment increased by 72% sequentially to $3.15b for a book-to-bill ratio of 1.33x, adding 6GW of capacity to the backlog for 25.7GW of new orders throughout FY25. In total, GE Vernova has 83GW in slot reservations for gas turbines driven by strong domestic demand. Along with infrastructure sales, GE Vernova is realizing strong services attachment that should drive long-term, margin-accretive growth for the market segment. Supporting growth, GE Vernova’s facility expansion support increased gas turbine yields beginning in eq3'26.

Wind Segment

The Wind segment experienced a strong increase in new orders in q4’25, likely driven by a pull-forward by customers seeking to establish wind infrastructure before the end of 2027 when renewable tax credits are set to sunset. The market segment faced some operational headwinds in q4’25 as a result of the halting of domestic offshore wind projects, resulting in additional accrual costs associated with the delayed construction. At the end of January 2026, a federal judge approved the continuation of the project. given the near completion of the project with only 10 turbine blades and 1 turbine needing installation, I believe GE Vernova should realize incremental margin improvements in eq2’26 as the organization phases out offshore wind in favor of onshore wind projects.

For eFY26, revenue in the Wind segment is expected to decline sharply in e1h26 before recovering in e2h26 driven by higher volumes and potential pricing power driven by contractual obligations relating to tariff pricing that will unfavorably impact e1h26. Looking beyond eFY26, I believe Wind should turn profitable as GE Vernova implements more dynamic pricing into contractual agreements, offsetting variable costs like tariffs and inflationary pressures.

Electrification Segment

Electrification was by far the leading growth driver with $7.4b in new orders for a book-to-bill ratio of 2.51x in q4’25, growing the backlog to $35b. Electrification growth was largely driven by investments by power infrastructure for utilities and data center equipment for the hyperscalers in the US.

On February 2, 2026, GE Vernova announced the completion of the acquisition of the remaining 50% equity interest in Prolec GE for $5.275b, enhancing its internal operations in the electrification market. Accordingly, GE Vernova is expecting to issue $2.6b in debt to finance the acquisition, though will likely maintain a healthy balance sheet given the $9b in cash on the balance sheet at the close of q4’25.

Electrification is set to experience substantial growth in eFY26 following the Prolec GE acquisition, adding roughly $3b in net revenue post-acquisition. In addition to this, management is expecting to double transformer and switchgear production in the coming years to better service heightened market demand, creating substantial growth tailwinds for the Electrification segment.

As a result of the tight market for electrical equipment, GE Vernova should realize appealing pricing power that should convert into revenue beginning in eFY27, driving margins for both Power and Electrification.

Tying it all together, I believe GE Vernova is positioned to realize substantial growth over the coming years as gas turbine slots are about sold out through 2029 and are expected to be sold out through 2030 when the backlog reaches 100GW of capacity. Beyond equipment sales, I believe turbine sales will deliver long-term, margin-accretive services agreements that can provide durable top-line growth while managing costs through inflationary escalators to protect margins. In addition to this, GE Vernova is in a unique position to cross-sell electrical equipment to essentially provide a nearly complete microgrid for hyperscaler power demand as well as provide a near complete offering for utilities.

GE Vernova Financial Forecast

Looking ahead to eq1’26, I’m forecasting GE Vernova to generate $9.2b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $2.03/share. Performance in the quarter will be underpinned by strength in Power and Electrification, which will include 2 months of Prolec GE performance, partially offset by headwinds resulting from the offshore wind project. For eFY26, I’m expecting GE Vernova to experience a strong uplift in adjusted EBITDA margins driven by pricing power for both Power and Electrification as well as improvements in the Wind segment following the completion of its offshore projects. Though I’m expecting Wind to substantially lag the other two reporting segments, I’m expecting incremental improvements as dynamic variable pricing comes into play.

GE Vernova closed FY25 with $9b in cash on the balance sheet and no debt, though investors should note that GE Vernova is expecting to issue $2.6b in debt in eq1’26 to partially finance the Prolec GE acquisition.

Risks Related to GE Vernova

Bull Case

GE Vernova provides a comprehensive service offering, including power generation, electrical infrastructure, and long-term margin-accretive equipment servicing. With strong demand for power and electrification infrastructure, GE Vernova is in a unique position of pricing power and the ability to increase production volumes in order to service demand.

Bear Case

GEV shares are trading at a substantial multiple when considering shares’ historical trading range. GE Vernova may face elongated headwinds for its offshore wind projects that may continue to pressure Wind margins for the coming quarters.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Management outlined a robust outlook for enhancing shareholder value for 2026, including a robust $10b share repurchase authorization along with doubling the dividend when compared to the previous fiscal year.

GEV shares are currently trading at a substantial premium at 72.81x EV/aEBITDA; however, I believe shares are priced based on future growth which entails increased gas turbine production capacity, increased electrical equipment production, and offloading offshore wind, all supported by orders strength and a robust backlog. Given the strong outlook, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for GEV shares with a price target of $1,169/share at 59.15x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Multiples Model Description

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

DCF Model Description

The DCF model can be read with the array of discount rates at the top and terminal growth rates to the left of the model. The forecasted cash flows are denoted at the bottom of the model. The share price array ties the respective discount rates and terminal growth rates. The highlighted prices denoted in the model associate with my expected range for intrinsic value per share.

data.description:
This article updates my review of August 2024 in light of recent performance and current holdings.

DHS Strategy

WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Fund ETF (DHS) was launched on 06/16/2006 and tracks the WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Index. DHS has a portfolio of 322 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 3.43%, and an expense ratio of 0.38%. Distributions are paid on a monthly basis. As described by WisdomTree:

The index is dividend weighted annually to reflect the proportionate share of the aggregate cash dividends each component company is projected to pay in the coming year, based on the most recently declared dividend per share and adjusted for a company’s composite risk score. Composite risk score is an average of three factors: value, quality and momentum.

The portfolio turnover rate was 38% in the most recent fiscal year and 47% in the previous year. I will use as a benchmark the Russell 3000 Index, represented by iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV).

Portfolio

DHS invests exclusively in U.S. companies, with about 55% of asset value in large and mega caps, 22% in mid-caps, and 33% in small and micro caps. The fund is well diversified across sectors, none of them weighted more than 20%. The portfolio has significant exposure in financials (19%), consumer staples (18.1%), healthcare (15.2%), and energy (12.6%). Other sectors are below 10%. Compared to the benchmark, DHS overweights mostly consumer staples, energy, and utilities. It greatly downplays technology, consumer discretionary, and industrials.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but company-specific risk is moderate. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 39.8% of assets, and the heaviest position weighs 5.8%.

Stock selection in IT and consumer discretionary contributed the most to relative performance versus the Russell 2000 Value Index. The largest contributor was Haemonetics Corp., a medical device company focused on products related to blood collection and management. The company is a market leader in plasma collection machines and has built a strong hospital business through several acquisitions in recent years. The increasing mix of this hospital business, which is higher growth combined with nice margins, should drive cash flow expansion in the coming years, especially as some of the acquired assets continue to reach scale. During the quarter, the company announced stronger-than-expected results, alleviating concerns that had surfaced in their prior print, and the shares quickly regained lost ground. Shares remain attractive from our reward/risk valuation perspective.

Another top contributor was Mueller Industries, Inc., an industrial corporation whose holdings manufacture vital goods for important markets such as air, water, oil, and gas distribution; climate comfort; food preservation; energy transmission; medical; aerospace; and automotive. The stock is beginning to appreciate the durability of the cash flow stream through a full market cycle, in addition to the modest exposure to data center construction that should help support margins for the next several quarters and perhaps years. Its balance sheet has more than $1 billion of net cash that positions Mueller well to continue its strategy of consolidating smaller niche players in the industry while also buying back stock and paying dividends. We continue to hold a substantial position given an attractive reward/risk valuation.

Stock selection in the materials and health care sectors were the largest detractors from relative performance against the index. Within materials, the largest detractor during the quarter was Eagle Materials Inc., a provider of heavy construction materials (cement, aggregates) and light building materials (wallboard) to residential and nonresidential markets. The U.S. housing market remains undersupplied, suggesting that lower rates and a resilient economy should support housing demand long term. Quarterly poor performance was driven by weaker wallboard demand amid macroeconomic headwinds in residential construction, despite resilience in cement and aggregates. Eagle generates substantial free cash flow, maintains an under-levered balance sheet, and has significant opportunity to create shareholder value through disciplined capital deployment. We continue to hold our position and view the reward/risk profile as attractive at current levels.

Another large detractor at the relative portfolio level was Alamo Group Inc. The company engages in the design and manufacture of agricultural equipment and infrastructure maintenance equipment for governmental and industrial use. Shares of the company detracted from performance as weakness in vegetation management continued to offset strength within industrial equipment. In response to ongoing weakness within vegetation, management has consolidated facilities in an effort to reduce fixed costs and improve capacity utilization. Encouragingly, bookings in the division slightly improved during the fourth quarter. We continue to believe the company remains well positioned to deliver shareholder value via capital deployment given its strong balance sheet and cash flow generation. In this regard, late in the quarter, the company announced the acquisition of Petersen Industries, a leading manufacturer of truck-mounted grapple loaders. Given an attractive acquisition multiple for the 25% earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization margin business, we believe the deal is well aligned with the new CEO’s strategy for bolt-on mergers and acquisitions.

DEFINITION OF TERMS:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund’s historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund’s performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund’s performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund’s standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund’s historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment’s performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager’s performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund’s performance and a benchmark’s performance.

Upside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

data.title: SharkNinja: Still A Buy As Market Share Gains Defy Macro Weakness
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered SharkNinja (SN), I covered their excellent performance, ramping up aggressively since their 2023 spin-off, being able to grow their brands while gaining significant market share and momentum.

Following yet another strong quarter and continued expansion plans, I'm reiterating SN's Buy rating, as the company remains in a very solid financial position and should be able to capitalize on the significant amount of data they gathered to take advantage of a potential consumer recovery.

Internal Developments

SN reported yet another strong quarter, beating the market's EPS and revenue estimates while delivering their 11th consecutive quarter of double-digit top-line growth despite the weak environment they play in. As we can see, their OCF grew extremely well, up nearly 42% compared to 2024, while the cash used for investing activities (mostly their CAPEX) remained almost the same, getting to a free cash flow of at least $474.35 million.

As for their guidance, the company expects a ~$40 million jump in CAPEX at midpoint, while their net sales would continue growing by 10% to 11% (vs. 15.7% in 2025) and the adjusted EBITDA would grow by an even better 11.8% to 12.7% (vs. 19.4% in 2025), while also announcing a $750 million buyback program - 4.33% yield, no expiration date.

Despite this weak environment, the company continues to focus on aggressively winning market share and innovating, with a goal of 25 annual new product introductions, with proven experience in delivering very strong market share growth globally and the ability to utilize their two brands to scale more and more branches (such as beauty recently).

Financially, based on SN's latest report, we can see a very strong position, with current assets covering their total liabilities very well, with a strong $777.29 million worth of cash and cash equivalents on the balance sheet and an ~11.4% increase in inventories.

Again, the equity is significantly smaller than the market cap, but it's important to note that this company's core assets are not physical, relying on their brands instead of actually owning the "heavy" production facilities, and I mentioned back in the previous coverage the gradual decoupling from Joyoung (the chairman's other company), reducing their cap rate significantly over time while he kept reducing his stake, which should be an improvement to their overall image for the market.

Macro Developments

Given the recent tariff reset following the Supreme Court's decision and Trump's subsequent increase to 15%, we could also expect a tailwind from this, as the company's guidance for 2026 assumes minimum tariff rates of 20% for China and Vietnam and 19% for others, although this continues to be a volatile and unpredictable situation.

Meanwhile, we can see potential pressure on the broader markets and consumers coming from the attack against Iran, the situation in Japan, and other factors mentioned before, which have the potential to lead to more weakness, fuel cost increases, general inflation, etc., coming at an already weak time for the consumer.

For example, virtually everyone has been reporting significant weakness in the US consumer environment, with consumer confidence plummeting to the weakest level since 2014 recently despite modest improvements in February 2026, while trade-downs can be seen in everything from the auto industry to apparel and even the most basic needs such as food.

Yet, despite all of these, the company continued to outperform and scale, with their CEO mentioning the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

Importantly, this performance came against a challenging macro backdrop. While our markets remained under pressure throughout 2025 on top of declines in both '24 and '23, we continue to rapidly gain share. According to Circana, the total U.S. market that we participate in declined in the low single digits year-over-year for the full year 2025, excluding SharkNinja's performance. Q4 was even more challenging for the industry with mid-single-digit declines year-over-year, again, excluding SharkNinja. The magnitude of our outperformance is noteworthy with market share gains across each of our 4 category groupings in '25, Cleaning, Cooking and Beverage, Food Preparation and Beauty and Home Environment.

To be fair, these trade-downs could actually benefit SN to some degree, as they are ultimately known for their great price-quality ratio, offering solid value for the price they come at, while the very high amount of data and knowledge they can gain from their rise in market share can offer important advantages that can allow them to adapt to consumer trends and "capture" the consumer with adjusted price points or products that fit their changing needs in an improving environment.

In fact, the company's CEO even said something regarding this during their Q4 Earnings Call, which is indeed a massive competitive advantage, in my opinion:

Our objective is faster, more precise product innovation and improved messaging and content to resonate in a stronger way with consumers. This is the SharkNinja flywheel. Consumer insights, a resilient global supply chain, always-on marketing and omnichannel distribution. We believe it's a competitive advantage that exceedingly difficult to replicate at our scale. The diversification shows up very clearly in our financial results, sustained double-digit growth, expanding margins and strong free cash flow.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, despite the company's strong performance, they'll continue to depend on the environment, and there are certainly scenarios where the demand for their products would fall alongside worsening economic conditions, renewed tariff pressure, and more.

Meanwhile, we shouldn't rule out the competitive pressure, as there's always potential to see another competitor do what SharkNinja did to the giants that ruled the market for so long, even though the company could also buy these if they are agile enough.

Valuation

For SN's DCF to Equity valuation, if we assume a similar midpoint increase of 12.25% for their OCF (same as their Adjusted EBITDA increase), minus the midpoint CAPEX of $200 million, getting us to a modest FCF of $511.8 million in 2026, without assuming any tailwind from tariffs falling. Note that I won't account for any net borrowings, as I'll assume that the company doesn't need to worry about repaying/issuing debt given their strong balance sheet, allowing us to capture their underlying potential.

I'll continue to assume a CAGR of 17.5%, which is not far from the market's EPS expectations (which I believe to be quite conservative given the potential margin improvements thanks to economies of scale) and not unlikely given their strong market position, significant momentum, data and ability to adapt, plus buybacks improving the final numbers on a per share basis, followed by a 10% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 3.5% terminal growth rate given their global operations and potential to focus on higher-potential markets like SEA, with a 10% discount rate to take into account a decent margin of safety despite their strong position and momentum, and 143.5 million shares outstanding based on their 2026 guidance.

This way, we get to an equity value of $15.34 billion, for an intrinsic value of $106.90 per share, which is below the current $122.87, still trading within a fair value range, while their FCF has significant potential to improve, especially in a better environment.

Conclusions

I reiterate SN's Buy rating, as the company continues to expand aggressively and remains in an excellent financial position, allowing them to take advantage of a potential turnaround in the industry.

Although they are trading somewhere in line with my fair value estimate, I'll note that this Buy rating is backed by their strong position and the other advantages named above, while their growth can easily beat these assumptions since we're talking about massive top-line growth that can lead to even better cash flow growth - which they've proven so far - as they scale and build their brands.

There are risks to keep in mind given the industries they play in, but they should be able to use their current momentum and data to adapt to the environment and continue their expansion - potentially in more industries as well.

data.title: Middle East Conflict: Implications For Global Markets
data.description:
By Jason Vaillancourt, CFA

Recent U.S. and Israeli strikes in Iran mark a significant escalation in the Middle East conflict, raising the risk of broader regional spillovers with global market consequences. For investors, the central concern is whether the conflict disrupts energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for global oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG) supply. A sustained disruption would lift oil prices, increase inflation risks, and alter interest rate expectations. Oil has risen to around $72 a barrel as of March 2, alongside gains in gold and the U.S. dollar. While Iran may lack the capacity to enforce a prolonged full closure, even partial disruptions—via shipping delays, higher insurance costs, or attacks on regional energy infrastructure—could keep prices elevated. The conflict in the Middle East remains a fluid and rapidly evolving situation. We are closely monitoring developments and will continue to provide updates as events unfold.

Key points

  • Conflict exceeds prior containment assumptions. Recent U.S. and Israeli strikes within Iran appear oriented toward regime-level objectives rather than limited deterrence, signaling a materially different and more expansive conflict than last year.
  • Energy supply risks drive the macroeconomic narrative. The dominant economic risk centers on potential disruption to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime chokepoint along Iran’s southern coast. An extended closure could propel oil prices toward $100 per barrel and significantly raise global inflation expectations.
  • Oil-driven inflation would complicate monetary policy. A sustained rise in energy prices would add upside pressure to inflation and interest rates, particularly in the U.S., where $100 oil could push CPI back toward 4%, undermining the recent disinflation trend.
  • Asia faces the greatest exposure, but buffers limit near-term shock. Although OPEC spare capacity (while limited and exists only from Saudi Arabia) and strategic petroleum reserves could partially offset lost Iranian supply, Asia remains most vulnerable to prolonged disruption given its heavy dependence on oil and LNG that transits through the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Markets are pricing tail risk rather than a base-case disruption. While Iran may lack the capacity to sustain a full closure of the Strait of Hormuz even partial disruptions—through higher vessel insurance costs, shipping delays, or targeted infrastructure attacks—could keep energy prices elevated and market volatility high.

Implications for investors

This is a fluid, rapidly moving and expanding conflict. As such, we will be monitoring the unfolding events closely and sharing updates. There are three critical figures that we are closely watching: the U.S. dollar, the interest rate on the 10-year U.S. Treasury note and oil prices.

  • U.S. dollar. As expected, the U.S. dollar is slightly stronger to start the week, but less so than we might have normally expected in a flight to quality amid heightened geopolitical risks. The Japanese yen and Swiss franc are showing strength as perceived safe havens.
  • Oil prices. U.S. near-term oil futures spiked to around $72 on the morning of March 2, while longer-dated contracts (extending to early 2027) moved far less, rising by only a couple of dollars. The strategically vital Strait of Hormuz—through which roughly one quarter of global oil and about one fifth of LNG flows, including a significant share of China’s supply—is effectively closed as Iran’s leadership adopts a more defensive posture. Major shipping firms have suspended transits through the Strait and rerouted vessels around the southern tip of Africa. A potentially prolonged disruption to global trade through key maritime corridors such as the Strait of Hormuz remains a central concern.
  • 10-year U.S. Treasury note. Relative to a week ago, the yield curve remains largely unchanged. The primary channel through which current developments are likely to affect financial markets is via higher expected energy costs and their implications for future inflation, which could constrain the Federal Reserve’s ability to continue cutting rates. We are therefore focused on monitoring key data and evolving risk factors rather than relying on historical traditional playbooks, which have become less reliable given the complexity and extraordinary nature of the current conflict. The range of potential outcomes remains wide, with significant uncertainty around the ultimate trajectory. Notably, there is still a risk of Iranian strikes on energy infrastructure across Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries.

Looking forward

For now, financial market movements remain relatively modest as investors adopt a wait-and-see approach. Risk appetite has held up well despite recent challenges. History suggests that while geopolitical shocks can trigger short-term market moves, a sustained impact typically requires a meaningful shift in underlying economic fundamentals.

Markets have often shown an ability to look through geopolitical episodes, particularly when fundamentals such as corporate earnings and economic growth remain supportive. These conditions continue to underpin market resilience. That said, close attention will be required in the coming days and weeks as events evolve and potential second-order and longer-term unintended consequences become clearer.

Disclosures

Geopolitical events — including military actions — may cause unexpected market volatility, disrupt commodity supply chains, and materially affect the performance of oil-related assets and emerging market securities. These risks may evolve rapidly and may not be fully reflected in market prices. Commodity investments may be affected by the overall market and industry- and commodity-specific factors, and may be more volatile and less liquid than other investments. Investments in a narrowly focused sector such as energy may exhibit higher volatility than investments with broader objectives. Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. As risk appetites are more balanced and investors become more discerning, the headwinds our process faced earlier in the recovery have lessened. As we move into the third phase of the recovery, we expect companies with durable asset bases, flexible balance sheets, and sustainable free cash flow to be outperformers versus the benchmark like we have seen in past cycles. In response to ongoing weakness within vegetation, management has consolidated facilities in an effort to reduce fixed costs and improve capacity utilization. Encouragingly, bookings in the division slightly improved during the fourth quarter. We continue to believe the company remains well positioned to deliver shareholder value via capital deployment given its strong balance sheet and cash flow generation. In this regard, late in the quarter, the company announced the acquisition of Petersen Industries, a leading manufacturer of truck-mounted grapple loaders. Given an attractive acquisition multiple for the 25% earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization margin business, we believe the deal is well aligned with the new CEO’s strategy for bolt-on mergers and acquisitions.

Fund positioning and outlook

The fund made minor changes to positioning during the three-month period. The fund increased its weight in the IT and consumer discretionary sectors while reducing its weight in the financials and industrials sectors as reward/risk ratios dictated. The fund’s largest overweights versus its benchmark remained industrials and materials. The largest underweights were real estate and financials. These weights were driven by our bottom-up reward/risk process as well as our portfolio construction methodology that is designed to isolate active risk to security selection as much as possible.

The low-quality rally that began in early April started to show signs of fatigue in the fourth quarter. This is not uncommon for the first phase following a bear market correction. The correction, which started near the end of the first quarter of 2025 and ended a few weeks later upon the "90-day pause," was very brief by bear market standards but nonetheless a correction. We expect the traditional cycle dynamics to continue as we move past phase one of the recovery and into phase two, which is characterized by return to quality factors such as earnings predictability, low financial leverage, and higher return-on-equity firms outperforming the benchmark.

The U.S. economy remains in decent shape and will be supported by the fiscal stimulus embedded in the latest reconciliation bill passed this summer. However, the current cycle is not without risks as investors monitor the weakening labor market. Fortunately, the Federal Reserve has the capacity and the resolve to react accordingly if unemployment rises. As that risk is realized, credit spreads will likely widen, lower beta stocks should lead, and companies with strong balance sheet positions should become more highly valued than they are today.

Since the start of COVID, the market has followed the oscillations of fiscal, monetary, and geopolitical changes by rapidly flipping from risk-on to risk-off behavior. What has been lost is the "normal" middle that traditionally makes up the longest part of the market and economic cycle. We believe that "normal" is simply a matter of knowing the direction of travel (fiscal and monetary) and the rules of the game (political and regulatory). With these questions being largely answered as we wrap up 2025, we expect "normal" is around the corner and the merits of owning competitively advantaged businesses that produce strong and growing free cash flow will become in vogue and the beta-chasing phase of this cycle will be behind us.

We expect the traditional cycle dynamics to continue as we move past phase one of the recovery and into phase two, which is characterized with a return to quality factors.

The first leg of the AI revolution has been focused on the neoclouds/graphics processing units/semis/memory and large-cap growth, but similar to past “revolutionary” advancements, the capital spending around the infrastructure buildout will benefit many other companies and industries, many of which fall within the value universes. The 2000–2010 decade saw a handoff from growth to value as the internet revolution buildout evolved. The speculative/non-earners have been the early strong performers in small and mid; however, investors will become more discerning and demand a return on invested capital from companies. Active management will be essential to determine who the winners and losers will be in this “sorting phase.”

ATTRIBUTION ANALYSIS:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. DEFINITION OF TERMS:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund’s historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund’s performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund’s performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund’s standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund’s historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment’s performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager’s performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund’s performance and a benchmark’s performance.

Upside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

data.title: Marine War Insurance For Hormuz Dries Up As Middle East War Intensifies
data.description:
Ships wanting to pass through the Strait of Hormuz are finding it almost impossible to buy hull war cover following the conflict between Iran and the US and Israel.

The Strait, a narrow stretch of water connecting the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is a key shipping lane through which roughly 20% of the world's seaborne oil and gas is transported.

"If you went to the hull market right now and said: 'I've got a tanker going through the Strait of Hormuz,' I think there is a possibility that you would struggle to find… underwriters looking to write that," David Smith, head of marine at insurance broker McGill & Partners, said in an interview.

The US and Israel on Feb. 28 began a sustained air campaign against Iran, which has responded with missile and drone attacks against both military targets and energy infrastructure in the Persian Gulf.

Iran has also threatened to close the Strait and said it will attack ships attempting to traverse it. While Iran's naval capabilities have been seriously degraded and few ships have come under actual fire, the Strait is already "de-facto closed given that a lot of insurance companies have stopped insuring transiting vessels," Bilal Bassiouni, head of risk forecasting at risk advisory firm Pangea-Risk, said in an interview.

"No major carriers are transiting the Strait," he added.

Price spike

For vessels travelling to the Persian Gulf that are not planning to go through the Strait, the price of hull war cover has jumped. While noting that prices are "highly subjective" and based on criteria such as vessel size, flag, ownership and cargo, Smith is seeing "rates of up to 1% [of the ship's value] for seven days without breaching the Strait of Hormuz."

Hull war insurance prices for the Gulf were around 0.25% of a ship's value before the fighting broke out, according to several news reports.

Shipowners also face paying more for liability cover if their vessels are travelling to the Gulf. Protection and indemnity (P&I) clubs — mutual marine insurers that provide ships' liability cover — have been cancelling certain war cover. All 12 members of the International Group of P&I Clubs, a risk pool that covers 90% of the world's ocean-going tonnage, have given 72 hours' notice of cancellation of parts of their war cover in the Gulf.

The cancellations are for non-poolable war risks, which cannot be shared between the International Group members.

"In most cases the clubs will be offering to reinstate war coverage at terms to be agreed," Peter Hulyer, head of P&I for the UK at insurance broker Marsh LLC, said in a press statement. "Mutual P&I cover offered by the clubs is unaffected."

The cancelled cover is typically related to war risks included the clubs' charterers' coverage, a range of covers offered to those chartering vessels. The move brings the P&I war market into line with the hull war market, Smith said.

Waiting game

The situation in the Strait of Hormuz means that ships are now building up in the area and looking for alternative ports, Smith said.

"The underwriters are waiting to see what happens, and I think most sensible shipowners are waiting to see," he said. "No shipowner wants to put either his asset, and more importantly, his crew in danger. They'll look for every alternative other than do that."

Docking at ports in the Gulf could also be risky. There have been attacks on the Port of Duqm in Oman, which Bassiouni said signals "a higher risk of collateral damage around other vessels that may be anchored in ports close to what Iran may deem as legitimate military targets in those waters."

Obstructing the Strait of Hormuz could trap some ships in the Persian Gulf, which could trigger so-called blocking and trapping claims under war policies, Smith said. However, vessels would need to be stuck for six months, a year or longer for this coverage to come into play, Smith said, "so that concern at the moment will be right at the very back of most underwriters' thoughts."

Red Sea risks

While Hormuz has been a focal point for marine insurers in the early stages of the war, they are also alert to the potential for the Houthis, an Iran-backed group based in Yemen, to resume attacks on vessels in the Red Sea. "Everyone is keeping a very, very watchful eye also right now on the Red Sea," Smith said.

Trouble in the Red Sea does not cause the same level of disruption as Hormuz because there are alternative routes available. Even so, an underwriter being asked for a price to go through the Red Sea now would give it more careful consideration than they would a week ago, Smith said. "We all need to be very concerned about what's going on within the Persian Gulf," he said. "But I think also you still need to keep half an eye on the Red Sea as well."

The level of threat from the Houthis will depend on how long the war lasts, according to Bassiouni. Following the 2025 ceasefire deal between the Houthis and the US, the Houthis' calculations will be more based on domestic considerations and their relationship with the US than Iran, he said.

That said, as airstrikes against Iran continue, the Houthis may eventually decide to "reactivate the Red Sea attacks to shift pressure away from Iran and also increase leverage that they may have at the same time," Bassiouni said.

data.title: SoFi: The Rebound Has Started (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Reasons to upgrade SOFI

Sharing a bearish thesis on SoFi Technologies (SOFI) in early December was a bold move because the stock was on fire at that time, which made my earlier bearish articles look not very good. However, the market sentiment has shifted towards more defensive stance and investors started repricing all aggressive growth stories in 2026. Thus, SOFI has been down by more than 30% since early December. After such a sharp drop, SOFI's valuation now looks much better than it did in late 2025. The company's PEG ratio has dropped to 0.79, significantly lower compared to the mature financial services sector's multiple of 0.97.

SOFI's low PEG ratio implies that it has an extremely bullish forward P/E ratio setup. If we delve into detail in terms of forward P/E, we can see that the projected trajectory of P/E compression is reasonable as it factors in aggressive EPS growth deceleration as large numbers kick in. For a rapidly growing business like SOFI, the estimated forward P/E for FY2027 of just 23 looks quite attractive. For example, in my previous SOFI article I highlighted that the projected FY2027 forward P/E was as high as 35 in early December.

I think that SOFI currently looks much more attractive because there were no disappointments from the fundamentals side. The company's Q4 earnings were stellar with another dual beat. Quarterly revenue surpassed $1 billion for the first time in history, increasing by impressive 37% YoY. Bottom line profitability continues improving as well, underpinned by excellent results in critical business metrics (members and products). Finally, the company also provided an upbeat guidance. Thus, fundamentals remain extremely strong while the share price has dropped significantly.

Moreover, the stock recently rebounded sharply from the oversold territory, which might indicate that we are witnessing early innings of a solid share price rebound. The rebound is likely to be supported by the fact that Wall Street analysts maintain their consensus target price of approximately $26-27 with no downgrades despite the recent sharp share price drop. It indicates that SOFI's long-term bullish case remains rock-solid. SOFI is currently undervalued by 42% if the share price is compared to the average Wall Street target price.

Furthermore, in my previous bearish call I highlighted quite aggressive insider selling as a warning sign. The situation changed in 2026 after the share price started tanking with a few notable insider buys recently. The latest one is a $1 million worth purchase made by the CEO, Anthony Noto. He bought 56k shares at a weighted average price of $17.88, and it was absorbed by the market with optimism. I tend to agree with this optimism because in November, the CEO was selling SOFI aggressively. This pivot might indicate that he believes SOFI is extremely attractively valued at the moment.

Robust secular bullish case for the entire fintech industry appears to remain intact. One of the global fintech leaders, Revolut, continues driving aggressive growth like SOFI. It is a larger player since it operates across major European economies while SOFI is focused only on the domestic market. In the current geopolitical environment of deteriorating relationships between the U.S. and its historical allies and due to President Trump's "America First" motto, I do not expect Revolut to expand into the U.S. market any time soon. However, it is very useful to keep an eye over Revolut's performance and plans to assess the power of fintech-related tailwinds because generational shift is applicable to the whole world. Recent report from European Business Magazine suggests that Revolut is likely to go public this year with a potential valuation of $150 billion, which will be a significant increase compared to previous valuation rounds. I believe that it is a solid validation supporting structural tailwinds for all rapidly growing fintech players including SOFI.

Finally, I think that betting on SOFI's rapid recovery is a safer bet than trying to buy the SaaSpocalypse dip. SOFI's attractiveness amid the current AI-related panic is that it is disrupting and rapidly growing in a mature and stable financial services industry. Generative AI companies can potentially disrupt various areas spanning software, digital advertising, or content creation. However, I think that it is almost impossible to imagine that OpenAI or Anthropic will ever apply for a bank charter and become a competitor to SOFI. What I mean here is that this distinction is highly likely obvious not only to me but to other investors as well. Therefore, sentiment around SOFI is likely to improve faster compared to major beaten-down software stocks.

Reasons against upgrading SOFI

The current geopolitical environment doesn't favor investing in growth companies. A big part of investors is currently obsessed with hydrocarbons and precious metals as there are various large wars simultaneously in different part. The U.S. is the largest oil and gas producer, but when other large players like major Gulf producers or Russia are involved in a war, such sentiment is understandable. Investors are currently fearful, and it is not a good setup for stocks like SOFI despite stellar fundamentals.

From the business perspective, there is an uncertainty regarding the effect of falling Bitcoin price on SOFI's earnings long-term growth potential. The company has been introducing various crypto-related products over the last several quarters. The management did not disclose the expected effect from crypto-related business on SOFI's earnings. However, since SOFI's focus is rendering financial services, it is obvious that the success of crypto-related services will depend on volume of transactions. With Bitcoin prices tanking in 2026, the interest from retail investors is likely to cool down. If major cryptocurrencies continue tanking, it might adversely affect SOFI's earnings revisions.

And even if the management did not expect rapid returns from crypto-related initiatives, they are likely expected to help maintain aggressive growth in number of members by attracting more people interested in digital assets. With that being said, even if there is no effect on earnings revisions, SOFI's actual member growth figures might be slower than expected.

The verdict

There are a few solid reasons to start buying SOFI despite the current highly uncertain environment. Fundamentals remain rock-solid, and the CEO's fresh insider buy might indicate that the stock is really extremely undervalued with notable upward earnings revisions ahead. Moreover, a sharp rebound out of the oversold territory means that a big rally might be starting and SOFI merits an upgrade to Buy.

data.title: U.S.-Israel Iran War Provokes Shipping Lane Shifts
data.description:
The US and Israel on Feb. 28 launched a large-scale, coordinated air campaign against Iran, striking a broad range of leadership, military, security and nuclear targets. A forced government change is now a key objective, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence country risk analysts.

Global supply networks may feel the impact through a mixture of energy market disruptions, airfreight challenges and container freight shipping network interruptions. In the case of energy, shipping via the Strait of Hormuz needs to continue; flows of LNG may be disrupted as well as crude oil.

Energy supply chain disruption

Absent an extended closure of the Strait or the destruction of liquefaction assets, the impact is unlikely to be long-term in nature. The Islamic Revolution Guard Corps (IRGC) is likely to expand targeting of critical Gulf energy infrastructure if US and Israeli strikes target Iranian critical national infrastructure and major crude export terminals.

Air freight disruption

Global air freight networks face challenges from the halt to flights through many of the regional ports, including the hubs of Doha and Dubai. These hubs handle around 2.6 million metric tons and 2.2 million metric tons of airfreight, respectively, or around 4.0% of the total global airfreight volumes.

The ability of air freight networks to adapt is partly limited by aircraft flight ranges, though networks can rapidly adapt, as was shown during the pandemic.

Container shipping disruption

Container shipping faces challenges to both local actions in the Strait of Hormuz and the wider region through shipping via the Red Sea.

Local actions in the Strait of Hormuz impact key shipping hubs for container freight, including Jebel Ali in Dubai, as we previously identified at the time of June 2025 conflict.

Container lines are also redirecting shipping away from the Red Sea once more. CMA CGM SA has ordered all vessels in the Gulf to proceed to shelter and AP Moeller Maersk A/S has rerouted vessels bound for the Red Sea around the Cape.
